subject: Timbs v. Indiana (2019), civil asset forfeiture, and the Excessive Fines Clause
central_question: The government can take your car, your cash, even your house by accusing the property itself of a crime — without ever convicting you. Where is the limit?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how a 2019 Supreme Court decision used the centuries-old ban on excessive fines to put the first real constitutional brake on civil asset forfeiture by the states.
surprise: In civil forfeiture the case is brought against your property, not you, so you can lose it without being convicted of anything — and Timbs is the case that finally said there is a limit.
stakes: Whether the state can keep property worth far more than the maximum fine for the underlying offense, and who that power is really aimed at.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in law, policing, property rights and how systems work
demand_evidence: 'Police took his car/cash without charging him' (civil forfeiture / policing for profit) is a recurring viral, high-search, bipartisan-outrage topic.
Self-relevant property-rights angle with durable long-tail interest.
competition: Most coverage is advocacy or news; few give a neutral 'how the system works + the 2019 constitutional limit' explainer.
differentiation: Present-tense, self-relevant framing (it could be your property); neutral and primary-source-cited; opens the 'what the state can TAKE' cluster after the 'what the state can SEE' surveillance trilogy (EP6-8).
research_feasibility: High: the opinion is public domain (586 U.S. 146); Cornell LII and Oyez provide the full record; the Institute for Justice documents the forfeiture system.
visual_feasibility: High: a vehicle being seized; the 'United States v. the car' in-rem motif; an animated scale comparing the $10,000 max fine to the ~$42,000 vehicle; a thread from Magna Carta's bar on excessive fines to 2019.
risk_notes: Decided landmark case (2019); opinion is public domain - advertiser-friendly.
Real person (Tyson Timbs) described by role, accurately and neutrally; sympathetic but factual; no real-person likeness in AI visuals (invariant 11).
Drug context kept modest and accurate (small heroin sales, addiction, guilty plea, no prison) — not sensationalized.

The 'policing for profit' critique is presented as a DOCUMENTED critique with attribution (Institute for Justice / ACLU), with defenders' view noted; framed as bipartisan, non-partisan. Keep neutral; consider an R3 expert pass on the forfeiture-system claims before publish.
Disposition is 'vacated and remanded' — the Supreme Court did not itself rule the forfeiture excessive; on remand Indiana let Timbs keep the vehicle.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding and key facts to the primary opinion plus one independent reference.
